title: Sida zuccagnii
intro: Sida zuccagnii, also known as the "Guajá", is a perennial plant species in the mallow family (Malvaceae) native to South America. The Guajá has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which have been attributed to its ability to reduce fever, alleviate pain, and treat wounds. The bioactive constituents of Sida zuccagnii include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the Guajá is often made into teas, infusions, or tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Sida zuccagnii is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malvaceae. It is a member of the genus Sida, which is a group of plants commonly known as the fanpetals or fan-leaved plants. The Malvaceae family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants that includes species such as hibiscus, okra, and cotton. Sida zuccagnii is native to South America, where it is found in a variety of habitats. The plant is often used in traditional medicine in its native range, where it is valued for its potential health benefits.
